data.title: Bavarian Nordic chair Debruyne steps down after failed takeover
data.description:
Danish vaccine maker Bavarian Nordic said on Thursday Luc Debruyne will step down as chair, days after a takeover attempt by Nordic Capital and Permira collapsed.

Debruyne will be succeeded by current vice chair Anne Louise Eberhard effective immediately, the company said in a statement.

"After careful reflection on both the process and the outcome of the recent takeover attempt, I have decided to step down to enable the board of Bavarian Nordic to focus on the company’s next phase of development," Debruyne said.

Last week, Innosera, a consortium led by Nordic Capital and Permira, withdrew its bid for the company after failing to meet the required level of support from shareholders.

The offer, announced in July, included a subscription price of 233 Danish crowns ($36.17) per share, valuing the company at around $3 billion and drawing criticism from Bavarian Nordic's shareholders. Top shareholder Danish pension fund ATP publicly rejected it.

Innosera later revised the minimum acceptance condition twice, to 75% and then to 66.67% from the initial 90%.

After protests from shareholders, it also hiked the offer price in October to 250 crowns per share and extended the offer period until November 5.

Bavarian Nordic's board recommended both the original and revised offers to shareholders.

Shares of Bavarian Nordic have fallen more than 20% since the bid was withdrawn.

data.title: KinderCare Learning Is Maintained at Equal-Weight by Barclays
Bureau of Labor Statistics: 'It May Take Time' to Provide Revised Data Release Dates — WSJ
data.description:
By Harriet Torry

The government has reopened, but the backed-up economic data hasn't started flowing yet. And the wait continues.

"We appreciate your patience while we work to get this information out as soon as possible, as it may take time to fully assess the situation and finalize revised release dates," the Bureau of Labor Statistics said on its website Thursday.

The agency also said it will announce revised released dates "as they become available."

The longest-ever government shutdown put the BLS behind on data collection and important releases, including both the September and October jobs reports.

Analysts expect the September report to come soon, since it is pegged to data that was collected before the closure began on Oct. 1. White House officials have cast doubt on whether a complete-or any -October report is likely.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Hogs Tumble Further, Steeper Losses Eyed — Market Talk
data.description: Lean hog futures on the CME close down 3% to 78.175 cents a pound. It's the lowest close for hog futures all year, and the lowest they've traded at since October 2024. Traders say that the most-active contract may have further room to fall. "The trend remains down ahead of further and possibly accelerated losses," says Dave Toth of RJO Futures in a note. Live cattle futures also fell hard, sliding 2.8% to $2.18875 a pound. It's the lowest live cattle has traded at since July. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Corn Futures Higher After USDA Starts Releasing Export Sales — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for December delivery rose 1.6% to $4.42 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Thursday, this after the USDA released export sales reports for the last two weeks of September, and also its schedule for export sales reports missed during the government shutdown.
  • Soybeans for January delivery rose 1.1% to $11.46 a bushel.
  • Wheat for December delivery fell 0.1% to $5.35 1/4 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Back to Business: The break ended for U.S. grain export sales Thursday. Some missed reports surfaced this morning, the first new export sales data since the end of the government shutdown, for the last two weeks of September.

This week's report showed data for the weeks ended Sept. 18 and Sept. 25. The USDA reported total corn sales were 1.92 million metric tons for Sept. 18, soybean sales were 724,500 tons and wheat sales were 539,900 tons.

For the week ended Sept. 25, corn sales were 1.42 million tons, soybean sales were 870,500 tons and wheat sales were 315,900 tons.

No mention of Chinese buying was seen in the report.

Blame It on the Rain: CBOT wheat futures couldn't manage to get the support seen in corn and soybeans, and were under pressure as global cash prices see weakness, according to AgResource in a note.

The firm said Friday's WASDE report is expected to make cuts to previously forecast corn and soybean yields, fueling upticks in both.

"The market has a large amount of 'yield worry' built into the price," said AgResource.

INSIGHT

Looking for the Evidence: The USDA said this morning that it will be publishing its daily sales of grains to private exporters that wasn't reported during the 43-day government shutdown.

Traders will be watching for this release, in hopes that the USDA says China was a customer for soybeans anytime from October to early November.

"We better see China's name on that report," said Brian Pullam of Linn & Associates.

Absence of China would likely introduce selling pressure to CBOT grains, particularly soybeans.

Setting the Scene: The WASDE report may serve as a barometer for how much stock the agency is putting in the ongoing U.S.-China negotiations, and the hopes of increased soybean export sales.

"If the USDA cuts it it tells me they do not have much faith in our 'trade deals' we are making," said Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting.

The USDA will publish the sales made to private exporters Friday, at the same time as the WASDE release.

Big Piles: Brazilian agricultural agency Conab estimates the total 2025-26 Brazilian grain harvest at 354.8 million metric tons.

Soybean production is estimated at 177.6 million tons, and corn production is seen at 138.8 million tons.

Conab also published its outlook for how planting for the next crop fared. Weather for soybean planting was seen as too dry in states such as Goiás, Minas Gerais and Mato Grosso. Low temperatures in October are also affecting freshly planted corn.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its November WASDE report on Friday, rescheduled from 12 p.m. EST on Nov. 10 because of the government shutdown.
  • The USDA is due to release the backlog of daily export sales left unreported during the government shutdown at 12 p.m. EST on Friday.
  • The USDA is scheduled to release its monthly Cattle on Feed Report at 3 p.m. EST on Nov. 21.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Gain on Winter's Approach — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas futures set a nearly three-year-high close as the market looks beyond milder near-term weather forecasts to expectations for a cold December. Nymex gas for December delivery settles up 2.5% at $3.646/mmBtu, the highest for a front month since Dec. 28, 2022. Record feedgas flows to LNG export terminals are also supporting gains. Tomorrow's EIA storage report is expected to show a 34 Bcf inventory build, according to a WSJ survey of analysts, leaving stocks 4.3% above the 5-year average. "We are nearing the tail end of injection season. As degree days build and LNG utilization stays elevated, the first sustained withdrawals should follow the next meaningful cool shot," Gelber & Associates says in a note.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Fitch Affirms Three Tranches of Trinitas CLO XIX, Ltd.; Revises One Outlook to Positive
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-13 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the class C-R, D-1-R, and D-2-R notes of Trinitas CLO XIX, Ltd. (Trinitas XIX). Fitch has revised the Outlook on the class C-R notes to Positive from Stable. The Outlooks on the class D-1-R and D-2-R notes.

Trinitas CLO XIX, Ltd.

----C-R 89642FAS2; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A+sf; Rating Outlook Positive

----D-1-R 89642FAU7; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; BBB+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----D-2-R 89642FAW3; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; BBB+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

Transaction Summary

Trinitas XIX is a broadly syndicated collateralized loan obligation (CLO) managed by Trinitas Capital Management, LLC. The transaction originally closed in May 2022, was refinanced in December 2024, and exited its reinvestment period in April 2025. The CLO is secured primarily by first lien, senior secured leveraged loans.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

Note Amortization Improves Model Implied Ratings

The Outlook Positive to the Class C-R notes is driven by the note amortization of the class A-1-R and A-2 notes, which resulted in an increased credit enhancement (CE) level and break-even default rate cushion against its relevant rating stress default level. As of October 2025 reporting, approximately 14.5% of the Class A-1-R and A-2 notes have amortized since the December 2024 refinancing.

The class C-R and D-1-R notes' Model Implied Ratings (MIRs), as defined in Fitch's CLOs and Corporate CDOs Rating Criteria, are three and two notches higher, respectively, than their current ratings. However, the breakeven default cushions were viewed as insufficient at these MIRs in the context of the portfolio losses to date and spread compression.

The Positive Outlook on the class C-R notes is based on the cash flow modelling results and expectation that amortization will accelerate as conditions to reinvestment became more constrained, which should outweigh the likely increase in concentration and potential decline in the credit quality of the pool.

Fitch conducted an updated cash flow analysis based on a stressed portfolio that assumed a one-notch downgrade on the Fitch Issuer Default Rating (IDR) Equivalency Rating for assets with a Negative Outlook. The portfolio weighted average life of 4.27 was maintained.

Asset Credit Quality and Portfolio Management

The Fitch Weighted Average Rating Factor (WARF) has slightly improved to 23.74 compared to 24.72 ('B' rating level) since the refinancing. However, the portfolio has experienced par losses of 1.1%, mainly driven by trading losses and defaults. The underlying weighted average spread decreased to 2.8% from 3.3%.

The rating action for the class D-2-R notes rating is in line with its model-implied rating.

The Stable Outlooks on the D-1-R and D-2-R notes reflect Fitch's expectation that the notes have sufficient level of credit protection to withstand potential deterioration in portfolio credit quality in stress scenarios commensurate with each class's rating.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Downgrades may occur if realized and projected losses of the portfolio are higher than what was assumed at closing and the notes' CE do not compensate for the higher loss expectation than initially assumed;

--A 25% increase of the mean default rate across all ratings, along with a 25% decrease of the recovery rate at all rating levels for the current portfolio, may lead to downgrades of up to three notches, based on MIRs.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--A 25% reduction of the mean default rate across all ratings, along with a 25% increase of the recovery rate at all rating levels for the current portfolio, may lead to upgrades of up to six notches, based on the MIRs.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

DATA ADEQUACY

Fitch has checked the consistency and plausibility of the information it has received regarding the performance of the asset pool and the transaction. Fitch has not reviewed the results of any third-party assessment of the asset portfolio information or conducted a review of origination files as part of its ongoing monitoring.

The majority of the underlying assets or risk-presenting entities have ratings or credit opinions from Fitch and/or other nationally recognized statistical rating organizations and/or European securities and markets authority-registered rating agencies. Fitch has relied on the practices of the relevant groups within Fitch and/or other rating agencies to assess the asset portfolio information or information on the risk-presenting entities.

Overall, considering the assumptions above, Fitch's assessment of the information relied on for its rating analysis according to its applicable rating methodologies, indicates that the information is adequately reliable.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

data.title: German coalition agrees to subsidised power price for industry
data.description:
Key points:
  • Subsidised electricity price set at 5 euro cents per kWh until 2028
  • EU approval needed for state aid compliance
  • Gas power plant capacity to increase by 8 gigawatts

By Rachel More and Andreas Rinke

Germany's ruling parties have agreed to introduce a subsidised power price for energy-intensive industries until 2028 to reduce costs and boost Europe's largest economy, Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Thursday.

German companies, including automakers and steelmakers, have long complained that high energy prices put them at a competitive disadvantage.

Conservative leader Merz said the subsidised electricity price for industry will be set at 5 euro cents per kilowatt hour until 2028, following coalition talks with his Social Democrat coalition partners.

"The aim is to significantly relieve the burden on our economy and reduce production costs," Merz said.

The EU must agree to the scheme under rules regulating state aid.

"Discussions with the EU Commission are largely complete, and we expect to receive approval for this," Merz said.

The VCI association representing the chemicals industry called the measure useful but said the government needed to do more to make Germany competitive for industry.

"We now need relief across the board - in energy, taxes and bureaucracy," VCI head Wolfgang Große Entrup said in a statement, warning that the situation for companies was "becoming more acute every day".

German voters elected Merz in February on a promise to boost German industry during a prolonged economic malaise.

The chancellor and his coalition partners also agreed on Thursday to tender 8 gigawatts of capacity for gas power plant construction and to reduce air traffic control fees — saving the aviation sector 350 million euros ($408 million).

($1 = 0.8575 euros)

data.title: Treasury Yields Rise Amid Increasing Odds of Fed Hold — Market Talk
data.description:
1542 ET - Treasury yields rise amid increasing bets on a Fed hold as the end of the government shutdown leaves doubts about the publication of relevant economic indicators. Trump's economic adviser Hassett says October unemployment rate may never be released because the monthly survey it is extracted from wasn't conducted. The data flow is expected to resume in coming days. The CME's FedWatch tool puts odds of a December cut as roughly 50-50, and no longer the slum-duck it seemed to be a few weeks ago. The 10-year gains 0.032 percentage point to 4.111% and the two-year rises 0.023 p.p. to 3.588%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1534 ET - The first post-shutdown workday hasn't brought any definitive updates on when the government will begin releasing backlogged economic statistics. But the BLS says it is working on a plan. The agency says: " We appreciate your patience while we work to get this information out as soon as possible, as it may take time to fully assess the situation and finalize revised release dates." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1106 ET - Price data collected by Homes.com showed moderate home price appreciation in October, with the nationwide median rising 1.3% compared to the same month last year. The median sale price rose to $385,000, up $5,000 from October 2024, but below the peak of $395,000 recorded in June 2025. The housing market generally lacked energy in October, Homes.com says, with lower mortgage interest rates failing to stimulate substantial buying activity. On the supply side, new listings rose 10.1% year over year, marking the highest October listing volume since 2021. Active inventory has now surpassed pre-pandemic levels, inviting buyers to take advantage of improved homebuyer affordability. The Midwest region continued to outpace other parts of the country, with year-over-year median price growth exceeding 5% in Cleveland, Chicago, Detroit, Cincinnati, and St. Louis.(chris.wack@wsj.com)

1103 ET - The euro looks set to rise above the key $1.20 level next year from $1.1637 currently, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a webinar. The eurozone should see a meaningful pick up in economic growth as fiscal stimulus takes hold, he says. A potential fall in oil prices could also benefit the euro. "The euro-dollar exchange rate's fair value in the medium term is heavily reliant on commodity prices though its terms of trade." ING also expects the European Central Bank is finished cutting interest rates while the Federal Reserve could cut rates by a further 75 basis points. It sees the euro rising to $1.22 by the fourth quarter of 2026. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1039 ET - There may be a little less holiday cheer this year, as financial fear is leading 28% of Americans to say they will cut their decorating budgets this year, Redfin says, with 26% reining in gift spending. Many people are choosing to spend cautiously amid economic uncertainty. More than half of those who are spending less on decorations this year say it's because they're trying to save money, while 44% cite economic uncertainty. Among those spending money on holiday decorations or gifts this year, roughly one-quarter are shelling out the same amount as they are on their monthly mortgage or rent payment on decorations.(chris.wack@wsj.com)

1023 ET - Despite White House warnings, October CPI and employment data could still be released in coming days, Citi economists write. The government shutdown disrupted data collection, but statistical agencies could ask retrospective questions in the household employment survey and use more imputation for the inflation gauge, Citi says. October CPI, however, could be "one with lower fidelity than usual." Citi economists expect labor data to remain soft, leading the Fed to cut rates in December, January and March. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1020 ET - The hidden, or additional, costs of homeownership now add up to $15,979 per year nationwide, according to a new analysis from Zillow and Thumbtack, climbing faster than incomes and straining affordability across the country. That's an additional $1,325 per month on top of a mortgage payment. Each yearn the typical U.S. homeowner spends $10,946 on maintenance, $2,003 on homeowner's insurance, and $3,030 on property taxes. Together, those costs have increased 4.7% in the past year, while household incomes rose just 3.8%. Insurance premiums have risen 48% nationwide since February 2020, topping $2,000 annually on the typical home. Insurance costs are rising nearly twice as fast as homeowner incomes. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1016 ET - National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett says that the October jobs report could be incomplete when it is released amid the government's reopening. Because federal survey staffers were out of work, the household survey that determines the unemployment rate didn't happen last month, Hassett says in an interview on Fox. Payrolls data could be salvageable, he adds: "We'll get the jobs part, but we won't get the unemployment rate." He says that some estimate of unemployment could be salvageable, but "we'll never actually know for sure what the unemployment rate was in October." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1001 ET - U.S. pending home sales fell 0.3% from a year earlier during the four weeks ending Nov. 9, a slight decline, but the first in four months, according to Redfin. The homes that are selling are staying on the market longer. Homes are taking a median of 49 days to go under contract, the longest span for this time of year since 2019. House hunters are hesitant because costs are high and the economy is uncertain. The weekly average mortgage rate rose to 6.22%, after the Fed indicated it may not cut interest rates in December. Also, the median home-sale price increased 2.4% year-over-year, the biggest jump in six months. Some would-be buyers are waiting for mortgage rates to dip below 6% before making a move. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1000 ET - The dollar continues to face weakness in the medium to long term, Validus Risk Management's Marc Cogliatti says in a note. The dollar remains overvalued even after falls since President Trump took office in January, he says. Trump has also been advocating for considerably lower rates. "Whilst that is yet to materialize, concerns about the Federal Reserve's independence have weighed on sentiment." Furthermore, ballooning deficits have long had the potential to destabilize the U.S. debt market, he says. While markets rarely move in straight lines and go through periods of consolidation, any dollar recovery could be short-lived, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 99.216 after earlier hitting a two-week low of 99.152. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0951 ET - San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly acknowledges that the Fed's balance sheet incurs a lot of criticism. That makes it all the more important that the Fed communicate clearly about its actions and intentions regarding its bond holdings, she says in a speech in Dublin, Ireland. "It's often unclear to the public why [central banks' balance sheets] are changing," Daly says. "This makes communication essential, acknowledging the multiple uses and, when possible, identifying how each is driving balance sheet growth." Her comments come as the Fed is navigating the end of its three-year balance-sheet runoff program, and facing the potential need to begin buying bonds again not to stimulate the economy, but to keep the supply of bank reserves in line with the financial system's needs. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0949 ET - The Japanese yen could recover by year-end as the Bank of Japan is likely to maintain its bias toward raising interest rates, Rabobank's Jane Foley says in a note. Japan's Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, an advocate of looser monetary policy, said Wednesday she would request regular updates from the BOJ. This caused the yen to fall on concerns about an erosion of the BOJ's independence. "It may, however, be too early to come to that conclusion," Foley says. The BOJ Act states that it must maintain close contact with the government so this is normal practice, she says. The dollar falls 0.1% to 154.57 yen after reaching a nine-month high of 155.04 Wednesday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Equities, bond prices fall as hopes fade for Fed rate cut in December
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall Street stocks fall with bond prices as rate cut prospects fade
  • US dollar slips while Treasury yields rise
  • Crude settles modestly higher, gold pulls back from 3-week high

By Sinéad Carew and Marc Jones

A steep sell-off on Wall Street dragged MSCI's global equities gauge lower on Thursday while U.S. Treasury yields rose, as investor hopes for a Federal Reserve rate cut in December faded after a chorus of hawkish comments from central bank officials.

In currencies, the dollar also fell despite the hawkish statements from multiple Fed officials, after the House of Representatives voted to reopen the U.S. government late on Wednesday and President Donald Trump signed the bill.

Investors had been buying equities in recent sessions in anticipation of a U.S. government reopening after a record 43-day shutdown, which disrupted food benefits for millions, left hundreds of thousands of federal workers unpaid, and snarled air traffic while putting a pause on crucial economic data releases.

However, Trump administration officials dashed hopes for a clearer view of the U.S. economy any time soon.

White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett told Fox News that, while the government will have a jobs number, data on the U.S. unemployment rate for October may never be available because it is dependent on a household survey that was not conducted during the shutdown.

And multiple Fed officials threw cold water on the idea that the central bank would cut rates in December.

Alberto Musalem, who runs the St Louis Federal Reserve Bank, on Thursday reiterated his view that policy is closer to neutral than to modestly restrictive, leaving limited room to ease further without becoming overly accommodative.

Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland President Beth Hammack said interest rate policy should remain restrictive in order to put downward pressure on still concerning levels of inflation.

And Minneapolis Federal Reserve President Neel Kashkari said he sees mixed signals from the economy. Inflation, running around 3%, was too high, he said, while parts of the labor market "look like they're under pressure."

Earlier, San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said the risks to the Fed's two goals are balanced now that it has cut interest rates twice this year. She added that services inflation has not been declining at a steady rate and that there was more concern that labor demand will continue to slow.

All this sent trader bets on a December rate cut tumbling to a 49.6% probability from 62.9% on Wednesday, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool.

"Markets were counting on a cut, and we may not get it," said Bob Doll, chief executive and chief investment officer at Crossmark, pointing to cautious Fed comments on the idea of a December easing of rates. "Most of them are putting up warning signs that it's not a 'gimme', just like the Fed Chair told us when he did this presser after the last Fed meeting. In some sense, it's not new, but people didn't believe it."

Anthony Saglimbene, chief market strategist at Ameriprise, said that, along with fading hopes for rate cuts, investors were worried about a continued lack of clarity about the health of the U.S. economy.

"What you're seeing today is a risk-off assessment. We're still going to get a cloudy picture on economic data," Saglimbene said.

And with the market already showing signs of concern about high valuations in heavyweight technology and artificial intelligence-linked stocks, he said it was not surprising to "see investors take a step back from risk, sell down the winners and go into the defensive areas of the market".

As of 2:45 p.m. on Wall Street, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 708.88 points, or 1.47%, to 47,545.04, the S&P 500 fell 108.91 points, or 1.59%, to 6,741.94 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 537.47 points, or 2.30%, to 22,869.43.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI fell 11.50 points, or 1.14%, to 1,000.28.

EUROPEAN INDEXES CLOSE LOWER AFTER HITTING RECORDS

The pan-European STOXX 600 index closed down 0.61% after hitting a record high earlier, while Europe's broad FTSEurofirst 300 index finished off 0.66%.

In U.S. Treasuries, prices were down, pushing yields higher, as investors scaled back expectations for imminent rate cuts amid lingering uncertainty over the inflation outlook and stark divisions among Fed policymakers on the trajectory of the U.S. economy and monetary policy.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes rose 2.7 basis points to 4.106%, from 4.079% late on Wednesday, while the 30-year bond (US30YT=RR) yield rose 3.7 basis points to 4.6986%. The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, rose 1.9 basis points to 3.585%.

Meanwhile, European financial stability officials were debating whether to create an alternative to Federal Reserve funding backstops by pooling dollars held by non-U.S. central banks, aiming to reduce their reliance on the U.S. under the Trump administration, five officials familiar with the matter told Reuters.

This helped to send the dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, down 0.36% to 99.12.

The euro was up 0.41% at $1.164. Against the Japanese yen , the dollar weakened 0.23% to 154.42.

In energy markets, oil futures settled slightly higher after selling off sharply in the previous session, as investors weighed concerns about global oversupply against looming sanctions against Russia's Lukoil.

U.S. crude settled up 0.34%, or 20 cents, at $58.69 a barrel while Brent settled at $63.01 per barrel, up 0.48%, or 30 cents, on the day.

Gold prices pulled back after hitting a three-week high earlier in the session, amid the broad market sell-off following the reopening of the U.S. government.

Spot gold fell 0.7% to $4,169.10 an ounce. U.S. gold futures (GCc1) fell 0.13% to $4,199.00 an ounce.

data.title: PLATTS: 100--New York pauses utility assistance halt amid federal funds uncertainty
data.description: (Platts)--13Nov2025/342 pm EST/2042 GMT **State pauses utility assistance disenrollment **Uncertainty remains over disbursal timeline New York regulators paused customer disenrollment from the state's energy affordability program, as officials said the 43-day federal government shutdown created uncertainty regarding the ability to disburse funds to low-income utility customers. "While the federal government reopened late [Nov. 12], that event has unfortunately created uncertainty about the federally funded Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program, known as LIHEAP, for the 2025-2026 [Energy Affordability Program or EAP] year," Anna Senatore, the deputy director of the Office of Consumer Services within the New York State Department of Public Service, said at a Nov. 13 New York Public Service Commission meeting. All of New York's large electric and gas utilities provide the EAP discount to customers who receive a Home Energy Assistance Program benefit. Most customers in the state are automatically enrolled in EAPs after their utility receives a HEAP grant on the customer's behalf, which the utility then credits to the customer's bills. The EAP program aims to provide discounts to 2.7 million New York households with incomes at the federal poverty level, according to the state. In the Nov. 13 order, the PSC directed the state's utilities to re-enroll customers who were disenrolled from the EAP program since Oct. 1 and to pause EAP disenrollments through Nov. 30, 2026. New York utilities in an Oct. 31 letter had requested that the regulators address the uncertainty of EAP funds. State regulators said the order was still necessary despite President Donald Trump signing a funding bill on Nov. 12 that ended the shutdown. "Uncertainty continues to remain as to how long it will actually take the federal government to disperse LIHEAP funds to the state of New York, for [the Office of Temporary and Disability Assistance, or OTDA] to actually open up the HEAP program, for those payments to be dispersed to the utilities on behalf of the customers in HEAP and then for those benefits to actually reach customer accounts," said Thomas Kaczmarek, manager of operations at the consumer services office. HEAP payments may not be credited to customer accounts for weeks, and possibly even months, according to Kaczmarek. Additional possible changes to federal LIHEAP funding also make the order necessary, even with the government open, Kaczmarek said. In June, New York City Comptroller Brad Lander warned that the city was on the brink of an energy affordability crisis, partly due to uncertainty surrounding LIHEAP funds. April mass layoffs at the US Department of Health and Human Services included the entire staff of LIHEAP. — Noah Schwartz, noah.schwartz@spglobal.com-- Edited by Meghan Gordon, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: LANDMARK BANCORP INC SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Landmark Bancorp Inc., a prominent financial institution, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant improvements in both financial and operational metrics, reflecting the company's robust performance and strategic growth initiatives.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Interest Income: $20.7 million, increased by 9.0% compared to the same period in 2024 due to higher average loan balances.
  • Net Interest Income: $14.1 million, increased by 21.5% from the third quarter of 2024, driven by higher interest income on loans and lower interest expense.
  • Net Earnings: $4.9 million, increased by 25.4% from $3.9 million in the third quarter of 2024, primarily due to increased net interest income.
  • Basic Earnings Per Share: $0.85, up from $0.68 in the third quarter of 2024, reflecting improved profitability.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Share: $0.85, consistent with basic earnings per share, indicating no significant dilution from potential common shares.

Business Highlights

  • Loan Portfolio Growth: The company experienced significant growth in its loan portfolio, with average loan balances increasing from $985.7 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $1.1 billion in the third quarter of 2025. This growth was primarily driven by increases in one-to-four family residential real estate loans, commercial real estate loans, and consumer loans.
  • Interest Income Performance: Interest income on loans increased by 11.6% in the third quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024, primarily due to higher average loan balances. The yield on loans slightly decreased from 6.43% to 6.37% during the same period.
  • Investment Securities: The company saw a decrease in interest income from investment securities by 4.9% in the third quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024, due to a reduction in average balances of investment securities from $428.3 million to $362.7 million.
  • Deposit Trends: The total cost of interest-bearing deposits decreased from 2.48% in the third quarter of 2024 to 2.18% in the third quarter of 2025, reflecting lower rates on deposits. However, average interest-bearing deposit balances increased from $936.2 million to $984.3 million.
  • Borrowing Costs: Interest expense on borrowings decreased due to a reduction in average borrowings and repurchase agreements, which fell by $14.0 million from the third quarter of 2024 to the third quarter of 2025. The average rate on borrowings also decreased from 5.72% to 5.09%.
  • Net Interest Margin Improvement: The net interest margin, on a tax-equivalent basis, improved from 3.30% in the third quarter of 2024 to 3.83% in the third quarter of 2025, driven by increased interest income on loans and reduced interest expense.
  • Future Outlook: The company remains committed to its strategy of growing its commercial, commercial real estate, and agriculture loan portfolios while maintaining a strong capital base. The management anticipates continued profitability and has declared a 5% stock dividend for December 2025, marking the 25th consecutive year of such dividends.

SEC Filing: LANDMARK BANCORP INC [ LARK ] - 10-Q - Nov. 13, 2025

data.title: Fitch Affirms OCP S.A. at 'BB+'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Dubai-13 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed OCP S.A.'s Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'BB+', with a Stable Outlook, and senior unsecured rating at 'BB+'. The Recovery Rating is 'RR4'. OCP's Standalone Credit Profile (SCP) is 'bbb-'.

The rating is constrained by that of its 94% shareholder, Morocco (BB+/Stable), given close links with the sovereign. We assess the linkage between OCP and Morocco as strong under our Government-Related Entities (GRE) and Parent and Subsidiary Linkage (PSL) Rating criteria. OCP's support score is 20, out of a maximum 60.

OCP's Standalone Credit Profile (SCP) of 'bbb-' reflects its large scale, strong earnings, market leadership in phosphates, very long mine life and strong cost position. We expect its EBITDA net leverage to remain below 3x through the cycle, despite large expansion capex and normalising fertiliser prices. The company's flexibility in growth capex will provide support to credit metrics during fertiliser market downturns.

Key Rating Drivers

Global Phosphate Leader: OCP's capacity expansion to 20 million tonnes (mt) by 2027, from 15.4mt, will reinforce its position as a global market leader. It will also increase its flexibility to produce different types of phosphates fertilisers, including triple super phosphate (TSP) that does not require ammonia. OCP's large and flexible capacity and its strong cost position globally will support structurally higher EBITDA, which we forecast on average at MAD40 billion in 2025-2028. We expect higher sales volumes to gradually mitigate normalising fertiliser prices. We also expect high EBITDA margins of about 38% over 2025-2028.

Large, Flexible Capex: OCP's projects are mostly uncommitted beyond 2025, which allows it to phase its spending according to market conditions, leverage and liquidity. We forecast growth capex will largely align with cash flow from operations in our rating case, as we expect OCP to maintain its balance sheet. We also project that dividend payments will be adjusted based on its financial results. The company will likely generate negative free cash flow (FCF) in the next four years, if capex is not adjusted to reflect market fluctuations.

Conversely, stronger-than-expected EBITDA generation would lead to higher growth capex than forecast to fulfill OCP's ambitious aim to increase fertiliser capacity to 20mt a year, build a green ammonia plant of 1mt, and generate 100% of its energy from renewable sources by 2027. It also plans to supply raw materials to the lithium iron phosphate battery market.

High Capex Affects Leverage: We expect OCP's EBITDA net leverage to average 2.8x over the next four years, but below our revised negative sensitivity for the SCP of 3x, as the company continues implementing its large strategic capex of USD10 billion in 2025-2028. This is in line with OCP's target of net debt/EBITDA of below 2.5x (before Fitch adjustments), with allowance for deviations during the expansion capex phase. OCP's strong business profile, high margins, flexibility in growth capex and commitment to the financial policy will support its deleveraging capacity.

Cost, Resource Advantage: OCP has access to the largest phosphate deposits in the world, representing about 70% of its reserves. This supports its expansion strategy, especially as other regions face resource depletion in the long term. Its production assets are integrated, cost-competitive and benefit from Morocco's renewable energy potential. However, ammonia and sulphur remain two important raw materials OCP has to procure from third parties.

TSP Focus: We view OCP's ability to greatly improve TSP penetration as uncertain, given the slow evolution of agricultural practices, and so do not forecast a large margin expansion. However, TSP affordability in periods of high ammonia prices, would improve relative to di-ammonium phosphates (DAP)/mono-ammonium phosphate (MAP), boosting OCP's margins. The TSP market is much smaller than the DAP/MAP market; however, as the new production lines are flexible, OCP could switch to DAP/MAP production, reducing the risk of under-utilisation.

Responsibility to Support: OCP's IDR is constrained by the sovereign's, due to strong ties with the state. We view decision-making and oversight under the GRE Rating criteria as 'Strong', given its 94% direct ownership by the state. The state controls OCP by approving its budget and investments, while most of the board of directors are appointed by ministries. We assess precedents of support as 'Strong', based on past financial support, including securitisation of VAT credits. However, the company has experienced had periods of high leverage due to large capex.

Incentive to Support: We assess OCP's preservation of government policy role as 'Not Strong Enough'. OCP is an export-oriented company and not an essential service provider, although it is Morocco's biggest employer and exporter and a sizeable contributor to the budget. We assess contagion risk as 'Strong' as its default could affect the ability of Morocco and other GREs to borrow on international markets. We view OCP as a reference issuer for the country, given its large borrowing on international markets.

Peer Analysis

CF Industries Holdings, Inc. (BBB/Stable) has similar asset concentration, single-nutrient focus and global leadership like OCP. It is focused on nitrogen fertilisers, which are a more competitive market. Its EBITDA margins are expected in mid-to-high 40% and its EBITDA leverage is lower than OCP's with about 1.5x expected in mid-cycle.

The Mosaic Company (BBB/Stable) has a smaller scale and lower market share than OCP but stronger nutrient diversification across phosphates and potash. Its EBITDA margins are weaker than OCP's, in the mid-20%, but EBITDA net leverage is lower, at below 1.7x.

ICL Group Ltd. (BBB-/Stable) is half the EBITDA size of OCP with a weaker market share, but it benefits from diversification outside of fertilisers with focus on specialties. ICL's EBITDA margins, in the low 20% range, are weaker than OCP's, but we expect EBITDA net leverage to remain below 2.0x over 2025-2027.

Saudi Arabian Mining Company (Ma'aden; BBB+/Stable, SCP: bbb-), and Fertiglobe Plc (BBB/Stable; SCP: bbb-) have the same SCP as OCP. Ma'aden's scale is smaller than OCP's, but it is diversified into aluminium and gold and has a stronger cost advantage with access to competitively priced natural gas. Ma'aden's EBITDA margins and EBITDA net leverage are comparable. Ma'aden's financial profile is supported by the absence of dividend payments.

Fertiglobe is a nitrogen fertiliser producer with cost position comparable to OCP's and with assets in the lower half of their respective cost curves as it benefits from access to low-cost raw materials. Fertiglobe has comparable EBITDA margins and lower leverage.

Key Assumptions

- Prices to follow Fitch's fertilisers price deck

- Total rock, acid and fertiliser sales volumes to increase to about 22mt in 2025, then rise to 25mt in 2026, 30mt in 2027 and 32mt in 2028, driven by increased capacity and a recovery of demand

- EBITDA margins to average 38% for 2025-2028

- Cumulative capex of MAD95 billion for 2025-2028

- Cumulative dividends of MAD26 billion for 2025-2028

RATING SENSITIVITIES

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

- Negative rating action on Morocco

- EBITDA net leverage above 3.0x on a sustained basis may be negative for the SCP but not necessarily the IDR

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

- An upgrade of Morocco

- EBITDA net leverage below 2.0x on a sustained basis could be positive for the SCP but not necessarily the IDR

For Rating Sensitivities on Morocco, see the rating action commentary "Fitch Affirms Morocco at 'BB+'; Outlook Stable" dated 29 September 2025

Liquidity and Debt Structure

At end-1H25, OCP had MAD23.3 billion in cash and MAD9 billion of availability under committed long-term credit lines, primarily a USD500 million five-year revolving credit facility arranged in April 2024, which remains fully available. Short-term debt as at end-1H25 was MAD24.6 billion, excluding financial leases.

We expect FCF to remain largely neutral as we adjust the capex plans to our conservative fertiliser price assumptions. Hence, flexibility in growth capex should help preserve liquidity, and OCP has historically had strong access to funding. Our rating case foresees that OCP will refinance some of its debt maturities in 2026-2028.

Issuer Profile

OCP is a Moroccan state-owned phosphate fertiliser producer. It has access to slightly over 70% of the world's phosphate reserves and is a top three global phosphate fertiliser producer and exporter.

data.title: Mercado Minerals Announces Upsize to Non-Brokered Private Placement
data.description:
(TheNewswire)

 Vancouver, BC, November 13,2025 – TheNewswire – Mercado Minerals Ltd. ("Mercado" or the "Company") is pleased toannounce that due to strong investor interest, the Company has upsizedits previously announced non-brokered private placement.

The Company will offer 27,990,000 units of the Company units of theCompany (each, a "Unit") at a price of C$0.20 per Unit for gross proceedsto the Company of up to C$5,598,000 (the "Offering"). Each Unit shallconsist of one common share of the Company (each, a "Common Share") and one-half ofone Common Share purchase warrant (each whole warrant, a "Warrant"). Each Warrant will beexercisable to acquire one Common Share (a "Warrant Share") at a price ofC$0.35 for a period of 36 months.  

Proceeds from the Offering will be allocated towards exploration ofthe recently acquired properties Copalito and Zamora, acquisitioncosts, the generation of new project acquisitions, investor relationsprograms, and general corporate working capital.

Finder's fees of up to 7% in cash and 7% in finder’s warrantswill be paid to eligible parties. Each finder’s warrant isexercisable at C$0.35 for a period of 36 months.

Insiders may participate in the Offering. The Company will rely onexemptions from the valuation and minority shareholder approvalrequirements contained in Multilateral Instrument 61-101 – Protection of Minority Security Holders inSpecial Transactions.  All securities issued under theOffering will be subject to a hold period of four months and one dayin accordance with applicable securities laws. The closing of theOffering is expected to occur in early December 2025, subject toreceipt of all necessary regulatory approvals, including from theCanadian Securities Exchange.

This news release does not constitute an offer of securities for salein the United States. The securities being offered have not been, andwill not be, registered under the United States Securities Act of1933, as amended, and such securities may not be offered or soldwithin the United States absent U.S. registration or an applicableexemption from U.S. registration requirements.

About Mercado Minerals Ltd.


Mercado Minerals Ltd. is a company involved in the
business of acquiring and exploring mineral properties in the
Americas. Mercado has been primarily involved in the exploration and
evaluation of the Porter Property, located within the Alberni Mining
Divisions of British Columbia.

UFC taps Polymarket to add betting signals for live fight coverage
data.description:
Polymarket has signed a multi-year deal with TKO Group Holdings to become the official prediction market partner for the Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC) and Zuffa Boxing, bringing real-time forecasting into live fight broadcasts.

A UFC blog post on Thursday said Polymarket will add a data-driven storytelling layer that tracks fan sentiment in real-time, including a “Fan Prediction Scoreboard” that converts crowd expectations into a live readout of the audience’s shifting pulse during each event. 

Polymarket is a blockchain-based prediction platform where people trade on the outcome of real-world events. The company's founder and CEO, Shayne Coplan, said the partnership will give fans a “new way to be part of the action — not just watching outcomes but watching the world’s expectations evolve with every round.”

The UFC and Polymarket will also launch a social series that highlights potential post-fight matchups across UFC’s major platforms to generate debate that Polymarket will turn into topical markets. 

Ariel Emanuel, the executive chair and CEO at TKO, added that fans will be able to transform “passive viewership into active participation.”

The rise of prediction markets

Prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket, which let users bet on everything from whom US President Donald Trump will pardon to the price of Bitcoin on a particular day, became especially popular following the 2024 US presidential election.

Since then, they have been expanding rapidly. On Nov. 6, Google announced it would integrate Kalshi and Polymarket into its search results as part of its AI-powered upgrade, enabling users to view real-time probabilities for future market events.

Polymarket also teamed up with fantasy sports platform PrizePicks to bring its event markets into the PrizePicks’ app, letting users make predictions on sports, entertainment and cultural outcomes.

Prediction markets have faced backlash this month. A Columbia University study reported that roughly 60% of Polymarket’s activity appeared to be wash trading and that a quarter of its total volume over the past three years may have come from artificial trades. The study has not been peer reviewed.
